title: Speaking & Listening
objective: Students will explore speaking & listening concepts including Performing Scripts & Poetry, Evaluating a Speaker's Argument, Summarising Spoken and Media Presentations and more.

steps.content: Today we're going to learn about Performing Scripts & Poetry. Get ready for an adventure!

Improvise, rehearse, and perform play scripts and poetry — using role, intonation, tone, volume, mood, silence, stillness, and action to generate language, explore meaning, and add impact to performance
Time to try this out: Perform a scene from a play using appropriate intonation, volume, and pauses to convey character and mood
When you performs in a play or reads aloud from a script, do they use their voice — volume, pace, and expression — to bring the character or poem to life?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Performing Scripts & Poetry. Perform a scene from a play using appropriate intonation, volume, and pauses to convey character and mood Use improvisation to explore how a character might respond in a new situation
Today we're going to learn about Evaluating a Speaker's Argument. Get ready for an adventure!
Summarise the points a speaker makes and explain how each claim is supported by reasons and evidence, evaluating the logic and relevance of the support
Time to try this out: Identify the main claim in a spoken presentation and the evidence used to support it
When you listens to a speaker making a case for something, can they explain back to you both what the speaker claimed and the reasons and evidence they gave to back it up?

Great job! Today we learned about Evaluating a Speaker's Argument. Identify the main claim in a spoken presentation and the evidence used to support it Evaluate whether a speaker's reasons logically support their main argument
Let's discover the amazing world of Summarising Spoken and Media Presentations!
Summarise a written text read aloud or information presented in diverse media and formats including visually, quantitatively, and orally
Let's practice together! Summarise the main points of a video or audio presentation in own words
After listening to a talk or watching an informational video, can you give you a spoken summary of what the main message was in their own words?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Summarising Spoken and Media Presentations: Summarise the main points of a video or audio presentation in own words Identify key information from a graph, chart, or infographic and explain it orally
Let's explore Drawing Conclusions from Discussion! Have you ever wondered about this?
Review the key ideas expressed in discussions and draw conclusions in light of information and knowledge gained from the discussion
Time to try this out: Summarise the main points made by different speakers in a group discussion
After a group discussion, can you pull together the main points that were made and explain what conclusion the group reached?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Drawing Conclusions from Discussion: Summarise the main points made by different speakers in a group discussion Draw a conclusion that synthesises multiple viewpoints expressed in the discussion
Let's discover the amazing world of Speaking Formally and Giving Presentations!

Use Standard English confidently in formal and informal spoken contexts — give short speeches and presentations expressing own ideas clearly, keeping to the point, and adapting register and vocabulary to the audience
Time to try this out: Deliver a 2-3 minute presentation on a topic with a clear opening, organised points, and a conclusion
Can you give a short prepared speech or presentation in Standard English — staying on point and adjusting their vocabulary to suit the audience?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Speaking Formally and Giving Presentations: Deliver a 2-3 minute presentation on a topic with a clear opening, organised points, and a conclusion Adapt language between formal and informal registers depending on the context and audience
Today we're going to learn about Adapting Speech to Context. Get ready for an adventure!
Adapt speech to a variety of contexts and tasks, using formal English when appropriate to task and situation while adjusting tone, vocabulary, and style for different audiences
Let's practice together! Present the same information formally to a panel and informally to peers
Does you naturally speak differently depending on who they're talking to — for example, more formally with a teacher than with a friend at the playground?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Adapting Speech to Context: Present the same information formally to a panel and informally to peers Adjust vocabulary and sentence structure when speaking to different audiences
Let's discover the amazing world of Multimedia Presentations!
Include multimedia components and visual displays in presentations when appropriate to enhance the development of main ideas or themes
Let me show you something cool. Select graphics, images, or sound that support and clarify main ideas in a presentation
When you gives a presentation at school, do they think about including things like diagrams, slides, or pictures to help make their points clearer for the audience?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Multimedia Presentations. Select graphics, images, or sound that support and clarify main ideas in a presentation Create slides or visual aids that enhance rather than distract from the spoken content
Today we're going to learn about Building on Others in Discussions. Get ready for an adventure!
Pose and respond to specific questions in discussions, making comments that contribute to the discussion and elaborate on the remarks of others
Let's practice together! Ask follow-up questions that build on another speaker's comment
In a class discussion, does you ask questions that move the conversation forward and add comments that build on what others have said rather than just repeating the same ideas?

Great job! Today we learned about Building on Others in Discussions. Ask follow-up questions that build on another speaker's comment Respond to a classmate's idea by adding supporting evidence or a related example
